policy_statement: Improve energy efficiency of data centres.
mechanism_description: Improve the energy efficiency of data centres to reduce overall energy use and related emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same digital service → ↓ electricity input (data centre kWh) → ↓ fossil generation → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Data-centre design and operation are improved so that computing, storage, networking, and supporting infrastructure (cooling, power conversion, airflow management) deliver the same workload with less energy. (2) Mediating process: Higher server utilization and more efficient IT equipment plus reduced cooling and electrical losses (e.g., better PUE, waste-heat management, optimized controls) lower total kWh consumed. (3) Climate outcome: Lower electricity demand reduces indirect (Scope 2) emissions and, where applicable, direct on-site fuel use, decreasing overall GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced electricity demand from data centres, leading to lower power-sector GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: 1) Dominant causal pathway: improved efficiency delivers the same data services with less energy input, which then reduces GHG emissions via reduced electricity generation. 2) Why this primary over alternatives: while the end impact is mitigation, the defining mechanism is “same service → less energy,” which fits Resource Efficiency as the primary category. 3) Edge cases applied: if a policy pairs efficiency with renewable power procurement, the renewable component would be mitigation, but the shared mechanism here remains efficiency-driven demand reduction. 4) Applies to all instances: any policy focused on improving data-centre energy efficiency operates through reduced energy input for equivalent compute output. 5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., cooling vs IT load vs operational controls) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the policy is framed as “green data centres” and implemented mainly through renewable electricity procurement or on-site generation rather than efficiency, the dominant mechanism could shift to Mitigation; confirm implementation details when available.
co_benefits: Reduced operating costs; improved grid reliability/peak demand reduction; reduced local air pollution (if fossil generation displaced)
instance_edge_case_notes: High-level bullet with no instrument detail; could map to a performance-standard or incentive-program if the underlying policy specifies PUE thresholds, audits/reporting, rebates, or procurement requirements.
